cataloging_subject.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous 1,794-line Middle English bird debate as represented by the Cotton Caligula A IX version encoded in U-M CME item OwlC. It is not the complete Cotton codex, the parallel Jesus College MS 29 version encoded as OwlJ, J. W. H. Atkins's complete 1922 edition, or an attempt to reconstruct the lost archetypal work.

catalog_summary.title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composition unresolved, approximately 1175–1285; the Cotton witness was copied in the second half of the thirteenth century
catalog_summary.region: Southern England is the broad conventional localization; the Cotton witness was copied in the south-west Midlands

titles.value: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this record from the parallel Jesus version; it is not asserted as a medieval title.

titles.value: The owl and the nightingale (MS Cotton)

scope.description: Title of the U-M CME item and pinned XML header

titles.value: ICH was in one sumere dale

scope.description: First line of the Cotton-version transcription

agents.name: Nicholas of Guildford
notes: The birds name Nicholas as their proposed judge. Modern scholarship treats his possible authorship as unresolved.

agents.name: John William Hey Atkins
notes: Editor of the 1922 facing-page edition from which the U-M/OTA electronic text was produced.

assertion.notes: No authorship proposal is secure enough to replace anonymous cataloging.
The poem praises Nicholas and proposes him as arbiter, but whether he wrote it cannot be settled. The pinned header correctly calls the attribution former.

scope.description: Cotton text printed on the even-numbered pages 2–150
Facing-page comparison of the Cotton and Jesus versions

assertion.notes: This broad range records the live scholarly dispute, not an evenly probable interval. The conventional 1189–1216 dating and a possible post-1272 date both remain in discussion.

notes: The pinned 1993-era XML header gives 1190–1210. That legacy estimate is preserved as source metadata but is not promoted as a settled modern date.

date_statements.display: Second half of the thirteenth century, in Cotton Caligula A IX and Jesus College MS 29

assertion.notes: This dates the two extant manuscript copies, not composition. Neither is asserted here as uniquely earlier than the other.
Southern origin is the broad consensus; the evidence does not securely distinguish Surrey, Dorset, Wessex, the Home Counties, or the south-west Midlands.

scope.description: Lines 1751–1794 identify Nicholas's home and the birds' destination

assertion.notes: The named destination is not by itself proof that the poem was composed in Dorset.

scope.description: Copying and scribal-language region of the Cotton witness, not a secure place of composition

assertion.notes: The British Library localizes the Cotton manuscript to the south-west Midlands. This physical-witness statement is separate from the poem's broader and uncertain composition localization.

form_statements.description: A continuous poem of 1,794 short lines arranged as 897 rhyming octosyllabic couplets.

assertion.notes: The British Library describes the work as a satirical verse debate, and Fein characterizes its style as lightly satiric; satire is therefore secondary rather than the primary genre.
The poem draws on natural science, bestiary traditions, and beast-fable allusions without being reduced to a bestiary entry or beast fable.

scope.description: The closing praise of Nicholas of Guildford, especially lines 1745–1778

assertion.notes: The poem sends the disputants to Nicholas of Guildford but ends before reporting a judgment.

summaries.value: A narrator overhears an owl and a nightingale contest the beauty, morality, and human usefulness of their songs and ways of life. Their wide-ranging exchange treats joy and sorrow, love and marriage, worship, labor, prophecy, reputation, and rhetorical skill. When rival companies of birds threaten violence, a wren restores the king's peace, and the disputants set out for Portesham to seek judgment from Nicholas of Guildford; the poem ends without reporting a verdict.

OwlC serializes only Atkins's Cotton text, printed on even pages 2–150. The Jesus text on the facing odd pages is a parallel witness with its own electronic item, OwlJ. The eighteen numbered DIV1 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups, not medieval sections.

extent.description: One continuous poem conventionally numbered as 1,794 line positions in 897 rhyming couplets. The XML contains 1,795 L nodes: 1,791 hold verbal text, three hold only asterisk lacuna markers at canonical lines 86 and 770–771, and raw position 660 is empty between canonical line 659 (‘Þe niȝtingale at þisse worde’) and canonical line 660 (‘was wel neȝ...’). DIV1 7 and DIV1 17 therefore each contain 101 raw L nodes, and DIV1 18 begins with canonical line 1702 rather than 1701; the supplied final hundred-line boundary is shifted without changing the conventional 1,794-position extent. The eighteen DIV1 groups, seventy-five printed-page milestones, and fifty-two Cotton folio-column milestones are electronic or edition locators rather than intrinsic work divisions; sparse L/@N values are checkpoints, not a consecutive numbering system.

content_parts.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Cotton manuscript version)
content_parts.summary: The complete 1,794-position Cotton-version poem cataloged by this record.

related_works.title: The Owl and the Nightingale
notes: OwlC catalogs the Cotton textual version of the work rather than claiming that the encoded wording is an authorial archetype.

related_works.title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
notes: The Jesus version is an independently copied parallel witness descended with Cotton from lost exemplar X; it is not a continuation or a section of OwlC.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/OwlC.xml, pinned CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: The pinned source identifies the Cotton-version electronic text, anonymous author, 1993 distribution layer, OTA U-1684-A, Atkins 1922 source edition, omitted front/back matter and notes, legacy 1190–1210 creation date, and the complete encoded poem.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of pinned OwlC.xml, performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1, L, PB, and MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Found eighteen supplied line-group DIV1 elements and 1,795 raw L nodes: 1,791 verbal rows, three asterisk-only lacuna rows, and one empty node at raw position 660. DIV1 7 and 17 each contain 101 nodes, and DIV1 18 starts with canonical line 1702. Sparse L/@N checkpoints support the conventional 1,794-position sequence but are not consecutive on every node. The file also has seventy-five PB milestones spanning even printed pages 2–150 and fifty-two Cotton folio-column milestones spanning 233r.1–246r.1.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item OwlC, ‘The owl and the nightingale (MS Cotton).’
evidence.locator: Item information and eighteen line-group contents links
evidence.summary: Confirms the public U-M item title, anonymous author, 1922 Cambridge publication, OTA number, lines 1–1794, current public-domain statement, and No Copyright–United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: J. W. H. Atkins, ed., The Owl and the Nightingale: Edited with Introduction, Texts, Notes, Translation and Glossary (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1922).
evidence.locator: Preface; introduction; Cotton text on even pp. 2–150; Jesus text on facing odd pp. 3–151
evidence.summary: The complete immediate edition confirms its bibliographic identity, facing-page witness arrangement, Cotton and Jesus manuscript examination, editorial intervention conventions, and the exact Cotton stream represented by OwlC.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A IX, record 040-001102334.
evidence.locator: Codex description and The Owl and the Nightingale at ff. 233r–246r
evidence.summary: The current catalog dates the parchment codex to 1250–1299, locates its production in the south-west Midlands, identifies the poem as a southern English satirical verse debate and one of two surviving copies, and links full digital coverage.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Jesus College MS 29.
evidence.locator: Part II and The Owl and the Nightingale, current ff. 156r–168v
evidence.summary: The official record and complete digital object identify the collateral Jesus witness, its late-thirteenth-century anthology context, current foliation, and complete public facsimile.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, text record ‘The Owl and the Nightingale.’
evidence.locator: Work, date, dialect, versification, identifiers, edition, and manuscript-base fields
evidence.summary: Provides a documented synthesis of the unresolved 1175–1285 date range, southern localization and its limitations, 897 couplets/1,794 lines, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, Atkins source edition, Cotton ff. 233r–246r, West Midlands manuscript dialect, and Cotton-even-page electronic-text boundary.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ed. and trans., The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II), Middle English Texts Series (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2022).
evidence.locator: General introduction; article 2 introduction and text; downloadable PDF pp. 74–157 for article 2
evidence.summary: Current scholarly support for anonymous cataloging, live composition-date debate, humorous animal-debate classification, 1,794 octosyllabic couplet lines, exact loci of both witnesses, their descent from lost exemplar X, and the distinction between a Jesus-base edition and the Cotton version.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for OwlC.
evidence.summary: Binds the catalog record to the pinned XML, U-M and OTA encodings, Atkins edition, Cotton witness, collateral Jesus witness, lost exemplar, complete facsimiles, access routes, and layer-specific rights evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be narrowed responsibly within the competing late-twelfth- through late-thirteenth-century proposals?
next_steps: Consult Cartlidge's full dating argument and subsequent responses rather than inheriting the pinned 1190–1210 estimate.
Keep composition, exemplar, and witness dates separate.

open_questions.question: Was Nicholas of Guildford the poet, the intended arbiter, a patronage addressee, or a fictionalized figure?
next_steps: Retain anonymous primary responsibility unless decisive evidence emerges.
Track Nicholas only as a disputed attributed author and named figure.

open_questions.question: Can the original dialect or place of composition be localized more narrowly than southern England without confusing it with the Cotton copy's West Midlands dialect?
next_steps: Review the competing rhyme, dialect, and place-name arguments in current scholarship.
Do not turn Guildford or Portesham references into a certain place of composition.

notes: Cotton Caligula A IX and Jesus College MS 29 are independent copies from lost exemplar X; the Jesus manuscript is collateral evidence for OwlC, not an upstream physical source of the Cotton transcription.

Atkins's older dating of Cotton to the first half of the thirteenth century and the pinned 1190–1210 composition field are historically important but superseded as settled catalog statements by current uncertainty and the current 1250–1299 manuscript catalog date.

The current OTA OwlC SGML is not textually or structurally identical to the pinned XML: it has an untagged canonical line 335, a duplicated line where the repaired branch retains an empty raw node, no final canonical line 1794, a 233v.2/233r.2 locator discrepancy, and malformed entity spellings. The current MEDictionary XML and pinned XML share the repaired 1,795-node/1,794-position stream. The exact transformation history remains unresolved and is documented in the lineage manifest.
